Ayahs

حمٓ (١)
Ha, Meem. (1)
وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ (٢)
By the Manifest Book! (2)
إِنَّآ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ فِى لَيْلَةٍ مُّبَـٰرَكَةٍ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا مُنذِرِينَ (٣)
We sent it down on a blessed night, and We have been warning [mankind]. (3)
فِيهَا يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ (٤)
Every definitive matter is resolved on it, (4)
أَمْرًا مِّنْ عِندِنَآ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ (٥)
as an ordinance from Us. We have been sending [apostles] (5)
رَحْمَةً مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٦)
as a mercy from your Lord—indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing— (6)
رَبِّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ ۖ إِن كُنتُم مُّوقِنِينَ (٧)
the Lord of the heavens and the earth, and whatever is between them, should you have conviction. (7)
لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ ۖ رَبُّكُمْ وَرَبُّ ءَابَآئِكُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٨)
There is no god except Him: He gives life and brings death, your Lord and the Lord of your forefathers. (8)

Content of Surah ad-Dukhan

This Surah is the fifth of the seven "Hawamim" Surahs (those beginning with Ha-Mim). Being a Meccan Surah, it fully encompasses their general themes—namely, discussions on the Origin (Mabda'), the Resurrection (Ma'ad), and the Quran. Its verses are structured in a way that delivers striking and awakening blows to dormant and heedless hearts, calling them toward faith, piety, truth, and justice.

The sections of this Surah can be summarized into seven parts:

  1. The beginning of the Surah with the Disconnected Letters (Muqatta'at), followed by a description of the Quran's greatness, with the addition that it mentions its revelation during the Night of Decree (Laylat al-Qadr) for the first time.

  2. Another section discusses Monotheism (Tawhid), the Oneness of God, and mentions some signs of His greatness in the universe.

  3. A significant portion speaks of the fate of the disbelievers and their various types of painful punishments.

  4. To awaken the heedless, another part recounts a portion of the story of Moses (peace be upon him) and the Children of Israel in their confrontation with the Pharaohs, their severe defeat, and their destruction and ruin.

  5. The issue of the Resurrection, the painful torments of the people of Hell, and the wonderful, soul-nourishing rewards for the pious constitute another part of the verses of this Surah.

  6. The purpose of creation and the fact that the creation of the heavens and the earth was not in vain is another topic addressed in the verses of this Surah.

  7. Finally, the Surah concludes by stating the greatness of the Quran, just as it began.

Since verse 10 of this Surah mentions "Manifest Smoke" (Dukhan Mubin), this Surah has been named "Surah ad-Dukhan."

The Virtue of Reciting This Surah

In a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), it is stated:

مَنْ قَرَأَ سُورَةَ الدُّخَانِ لَيْلَةَ الْجُمُعَةِ وَ يَوْمَ الْجُمُعَةِ بَنَى اللَّهُ لَهُ بَيْتاً فِي الْجَنَّةِ:

"Whoever recites Surah ad-Dukhan on the night and day of Friday, Allah will build a house for him in Paradise." [^1] It is also narrated from him:

مَنْ قَرَأَ سُورَةَ الدُّخَانِ فِي لَيْلَةٍ، أَصْبَحَ يَسْتَغْفِرُ لَهُ سَبْعُونَ أَلْفَ مَلَكٍ:

"Whoever recites Surah ad-Dukhan in a night, he shall reach the morning while seventy thousand angels seek forgiveness for him." [^2] In another Hadith from Abu Hamza al-Thumali, it is narrated from Imam al-Baqir (peace be upon him):

مَنْ قَرَأَ سُورَةَ الدُّخَانِ فِي فَرَائِضِهِ وَ نَوَافِلِهِ بَعَثَهُ اللَّهُ مِنَ الْآمِنِينَ يَوْمَ الْقِيَامَةِ، وَ أَظَلَّهُ تَحْتَ ظِلِّ عَرْشِهِ، وَ حَاسَبَهُ حِسَاباً يَسِيراً، وَ أَعْطَى كِتَابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ:

"Whoever recites Surah ad-Dukhan in his obligatory and supererogatory prayers, Allah will resurrect him among those who are in security on the Day of Resurrection, shade him under the shade of His Throne, grant him an easy reckoning, and give him his record of deeds in his right hand." ^3

[^1] and [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, beginning of Surah ad-Dukhan.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

حم (1) وَ الْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ (2) إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ إِنَّا كُنَّا مُنْذِرِينَ (3) فِيها يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ (4)

أَمْراً مِنْ عِنْدِنا إِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ (5) رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ (6) رَبِّ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُوقِنِينَ (7) لا إِلهَ إِلاَّ هُوَ يُحْيِي وَ يُمِيتُ رَبُّكُمْ وَ رَبُّ آبائِكُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ (8)

Commentary

The Revelation of the Quran on a Blessed Night

At the beginning of this Surah, just like the previous four Surahs and the two following ones—which together form a group of seven—we encounter the Disconnected Letters (Ha-Mim). Regarding the interpretation of the Disconnected Letters in general, we have discussed them extensively in the past [^1], and specifically regarding "Ha-Mim," it was discussed at the beginning of the first of the Hawamim Surahs (Surah al-Mu'min/Ghafir) and the beginning of Surah Fussilat.

It is noteworthy that some commentators have interpreted "Ha-Mim" here as an oath, which, together with the subsequent oath sworn by the Quran, forms two consecutive and harmonious oaths: an oath by the letters of the alphabet like Ha-Mim, and an oath by this Holy Book which is composed of these letters.

[^1]: Refer to Volume 1 at the beginning of Surah al-Baqarah, Volume 2 at the beginning of Surah Al-Imran, and Volume 6 at the beginning of Surah al-A'raf.

In the second verse of this Surah, as mentioned, an oath is taken by the "Glorious Quran," stating: "By the manifest Book" (وَ الْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ). A book whose content is clear, its knowledge manifest, its teachings vibrant, its laws constructive, and its programs calculated; a book that is itself the proof of its own truthfulness—"The sun has come as the proof of the sun." [^1]

But let us see for what purpose this oath was mentioned. The following verse clarifies this reality, saying: "Indeed, We sent it down (which is the document of the truthfulness of the Prophet of Islam) on a blessed night" (إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ). "Mubarak" (Blessed) is derived from the root "Barakah," meaning beneficial, eternal, and enduring.

Which night is this that is the origin of blessings and the source of lasting goodness?

Most commentators have interpreted it as the Night of Decree (Laylat al-Qadr)—the blessed night in which the destiny of humanity took on a new color with the revelation of the Quran; the night in which the fates of the creation and uniform decrees are ordained. Yes, the Quran was revealed to the pure heart of the Prophet on a destiny-shaping night.

It is necessary to note that the apparent meaning of the verse is that the entire Quran was revealed on the Night of Decree.

[^1]: Regarding the philosophy of the Quranic oaths and their primary objective, we will discuss this, God willing, in the commentary of the final part (Juz') of the Quran under the many verses where repeated oaths are found.

But what was the primary goal of its revelation? It is what is pointed out at the end of this same verse: "Indeed, We were ever warning" (إِنَّا كُنَّا مُنْذِرِينَ).

This is Our perpetual tradition: that We commission Our messengers to warn the oppressors and polytheists, and the sending of the Prophet of Islam with this manifest Book is the final link in this chain.

It is true that prophets warn on one hand and give glad tidings on the other, but since the primary foundation of their call in the face of an oppressive and criminal people mostly consists of warning and frightening, it is often mentioned in this way.

The Instantaneous Revelation and the Gradual Revelation of the Quran

  1. We know that the Quran was revealed over twenty-three years during the Prophet's mission. Furthermore, the content of the Quran is linked to various events in the life of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and the Muslims throughout these 23 years; if severed from them, it would be incomprehensible.

Given this, how could the Quran have been revealed in its entirety on the Night of Decree?

In response to this question, some have interpreted "revelation" as the beginning of the Quran's revelation. Thus, there is no obstacle to its beginning being on the Night of Decree, with its continuation spanning 23 years.

However, as we said, this interpretation is not consistent with the apparent meaning of the verse under discussion and other verses of the Holy Quran.

To find the answer to this question, one must note that on one hand, we read in the verse: "The Quran was revealed on a blessed night."

On the other hand, in verse 185 of Surah al-Baqarah, it says: شَهْرُ رَمَضانَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ فِيهِ الْقُرْآنُ "The month of Ramadan is that in which the Quran was revealed."

And thirdly, in Surah al-Qadr, we read: إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ "Indeed, We sent it down on the Night of Decree."

From the sum of these verses, it is clearly understood that the blessed night mentioned in the verse under discussion is the Night of Decree in the holy month of Ramadan.

Beyond this, it is understood from several verses that the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) was aware of the Quran before its gradual revelation, such as verse 114 of Surah Ta-Ha: وَ لا تَعْجَلْ بِالْقُرْآنِ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ يُقْضى‌ إِلَيْكَ وَحْيُهُ "...and do not hasten with the Quran before its revelation is completed to you."

And in verse 16 of Surah al-Qiyamah, it says: لا تُحَرِّكْ بِهِ لِسانَكَ لِتَعْجَلَ بِهِ "Move not your tongue with it to hasten it."

From the sum of these verses, it can be concluded that the Quran had two types of revelation: First, "Instantaneous Revelation" (Inzal), where it was revealed all at once from Allah upon the pure heart of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) in the month of Ramadan and the Night of Decree. Second, "Gradual Revelation" (Tanzil), which was revealed according to conditions, events, and needs over 23 years.

Another piece of evidence for this is that in some verses of the Quran, the term "Inzal" is used, while in others, "Tanzil" is used. It is understood from some linguistic texts that "Tanzil" is usually used in cases where something is revealed gradually and in a scattered manner, whereas "Inzal" has a broad meaning that includes both gradual and instantaneous revelation. [^1] Interestingly, in all the above verses speaking of the Quran's revelation on the Night of Decree and the month of Ramadan, the term "Inzal" is used, which is consistent with "instantaneous revelation," whereas

[^1]: Refer to Raghib's Mufradat, root "N-Z-L".

in other cases where "gradual revelation" is discussed, only the term "Tanzil" is used.

But how was this "instantaneous revelation" upon the heart of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family)? Was it in the form of this current Quran with its various verses and Surahs?

Or were its concepts and realities revealed in a compressed and collective form?

It is not precisely clear; we only understand from the aforementioned clues that this Quran was revealed once in a single night upon the pure heart of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), and once gradually over 23 years.

Another proof for this is that the term "Quran" in the above verse appears to refer to the Quran as a whole. It is true that the word "Quran" is applied to both the "whole" and the "part," but it cannot be denied that the apparent meaning of this word, when no other clue accompanies it, is the entire Quran. Thus, the interpretation by some that the verse refers to the beginning of the Quran's revelation—saying the first verses were revealed in Ramadan and the Night of Decree—is contrary to the apparent meaning of the verses.

Even weaker is the view of those who say: because Surah al-Hamd (al-Fatiha), which is the essence and summary of the entire Quran, was revealed on the Night of Decree, the expression إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةِ الْقَدْرِ was used! All these possibilities contradict the apparent meaning of the verses, for the apparent meaning is that the entire Quran was revealed on the Night of Decree.

The only thing that remains here is that in several narrations cited in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim from Imam al-Baqir, Imam al-Sadiq, and Imam Abu al-Hasan (Musa ibn Ja'far) (peace be upon them), we read regarding the interpretation of إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ that they said:

"It is the Night of Decree; Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, sent down the Quran in it to the 'Inhabited House' (al-Bayt al-Ma'mur) all at once, then it was revealed from the Inhabited House upon the Messenger of Allah (peace be upon him and his family) over the course of twenty years." (Note that here too, "Inzal" is used for the instantaneous revelation and "Nazzala" for the gradual revelation). [^1] Regarding where the "Inhabited House" is, several narrations—the explanation of which will come, God willing, under verse 4 of Surah at-Tur—state that it is a house in the heavens directly above the Kaaba, which is the place of worship for the angels; every day seventy thousand angels enter it and do not return to it until the Day of Resurrection.

However, as to which heaven the "Inhabited House" is in, narrations vary. In many of them, it is the fourth heaven; in some, the first heaven (the lowest heaven); and in some, the seventh heaven. In a Hadith that the late Tabarsi cited in "Majma' al-Bayan" in the commentary of Surah at-Tur from Ali (peace be upon him), we read:

"It is a house in the fourth heaven corresponding to the Kaaba, inhabited by the angels through the worship they perform therein; every day seventy thousand angels enter it and never return to it!" [^2] In any case, the revelation of the Quran in its entirety on the Night of Decree to the Inhabited House never

[^1]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 620. In this Hadith, the gradual revelation of the Quran is mentioned as twenty years, while we know the period of prophethood in which the Quran was revealed was twenty-three years. It is possible this expression is due to a narrator's error or a mistake in the manuscripts of the Hadith.

[^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 163. The late Allamah Majlisi in Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 58, p. 55 onwards, has collected narrations related to the "Inhabited House."

contradicts the Prophet's (peace be upon him and his family) awareness of it, for although he does not have access to the Preserved Tablet (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz) which is the hidden knowledge of God, the Imam [and Prophet] is aware of other realms.

In other words, what we inferred from the previous verses—that the Quran was revealed twice to the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family), once as an "instantaneous revelation" on the Night of Decree and another time as a "gradual revelation" over 23 years—does not contradict the above Hadith which says it was revealed to the Inhabited House on the Night of Decree. This is because the heart of the Prophet is aware of the "Inhabited House."

With what has been said in response to this question, the answer to another question—which asks: "If the Quran was revealed on the Night of Decree, then how, according to famous narrations, did the beginning of the Prophet's mission occur on the 27th of Rajab?"—becomes clear.

For its revelation in the month of Ramadan was of a collective nature, while the revelation of the first verses on the 27th of Rajab pertains to its gradual revelation, and thus no problem arises in this regard.

The next verse is a description and explanation for the Night of Decree, saying: "On that night every precise matter is made distinct" (فِيها يُفْرَقُ كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ). The expression "yufraqu" (is made distinct) refers to the fact that all destiny-shaping matters and issues are ordained on that night, and the term "hakim" (precise/wise) expresses the firmness of this divine decree, its immutability, and its wisdom. However, this attribute in the Quran is usually mentioned for Allah, but describing other matters with it is for the sake of emphasis. [^1] This statement is consistent with many narrations stating that on the Night of Decree, the destinies

[^1]: In "Al-Mizan," another interpretation for this verse is mentioned, the summary of which is: the affairs of this world have two stages: a stage of brevity and ambiguity, referred to as "hakim," and a stage of detail and multiplicity, referred to as "yufraqu" (Vol. 18, p. 140).

of all humans for one year are determined, and provisions, life spans, and other matters are distinguished and clarified on that night.

[Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 21, p. 153]

The details of this discussion and other issues regarding the Night of Decree, and its lack of conflict with human free will, will be discussed extensively, God willing, in the commentary of "Surah al-Qadr."

In the following verse, to emphasize that the Quran is from Allah, He says:

"The revelation of the Quran on the Night of Decree was a command from Us, and indeed, We have been sending [messengers]" (أَمْراً مِنْ عِنْدِنا إِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ). [^1]

Then, to state the primary reason for the revelation of the Quran, the sending of the Prophet, and the decrees of the Night of Decree, He adds: "All this is as a mercy from your Lord" (رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ). [^2] Yes, His infinite mercy necessitates that He does not leave His servants to themselves, but sends them a program and a guide to lead them through the winding path of perfection and the journey toward Allah. Fundamentally, the entire universe has originated from

[^1]: Regarding the grammatical position of the phrase أَمْراً مِنْ عِنْدِنا... and which part of the previous verses it refers to, various possibilities have been given. It is most appropriate that "أَمْراً مِنْ عِنْدِنا" is a circumstantial clause (hal) for the object pronoun in "Inna anzalnahu," meaning: We sent the Quran while it is a command from Us. In this case, it is perfectly harmonious with the phrase إِنَّا كُنَّا مُرْسِلِينَ which speaks of sending the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family). There is also the possibility that it is an explanation for "كُلُّ أَمْرٍ حَكِيمٍ," and its accusative case is due to specification (ikhtisas), meaning: I mean by this matter a matter originating from Us.

[^2]: "رَحْمَةً مِنْ رَبِّكَ" is an "object of purpose" (maf'ul la-ajlih) for "Inna anzalnahu" or for "yufraqu kullu amrin hakim," or both.

His unstinting mercy, and humans are the most included in this mercy.

At the end of this verse and the following verses, He enumerates seven attributes for Allah, all of which express His station of Monotheism (Tawhid), saying: "Indeed, He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing" (إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ).

He hears the requests of His servants and is aware of the secrets within their hearts.

Then, in stating the third description, He says: "The Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, if you should be certain" (رَبِّ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُوقِنِينَ). [^1] [^2] Since many polytheists believed in multiple gods and lords, imagining a lord for every type of being, the expression "your Lord" in the previous verse might have created the illusion for them that the Prophet's Lord is different from the Lord of other beings. In this verse, with the phrase "Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them," He strikes a line of cancellation through all of them and proves that the Lord of all beings in the universe is One.

The phrase "إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُوقِنِينَ" (if you should be certain), which appears as a conditional sentence, raises a question here: Is the Lordship of the Lord of the universe conditional upon such a condition?

But it appears that the purpose of mentioning this phrase is to express one of two meanings, or both: First, "If you are seekers of certainty, the way is to reflect upon the absolute Lordship of the Lord."

Second, "If you are people of certainty, this is the best place to find certainty." If you, who see the signs of Allah's Lordship throughout the entire universe, and find a sign of this Lordship in every atom you split, do not gain certainty in His Lordship, what else in the world can you find faith and certainty in?!

In the fourth, fifth, and sixth descriptions, He says: "There is no deity except Him; He gives life and causes death" (لا إِلهَ إِلَّا هُوَ يُحْيِي وَ يُمِيتُ). [^1] Your life and death are in His hands, and He is your Lord and the Lord of all the worlds; therefore, no deity can exist except Him. Can one who has neither the station of Lordship nor ownership of life and death be a deity?! And in the seventh and final description, He adds: "[He is] your Lord and the Lord of your first forefathers" (وَ رَبُّ آبائِكُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ).

If you say you worship idols because your ancestors worshipped them, know that their Lord was also the One and Only God, and your affection for your ancestors also necessitates that you bow your head only at the threshold of the One God; and if they had a way or custom other than this, they were certainly in error.

It is clear that the issue of life and death is among the affairs of the Lord's governance. If He mentions it specifically, it is both because of its extraordinary importance and an implicit reference to the issue of the Resurrection. This is not the first time the Quran emphasizes the issue of life and death; rather, it has repeatedly stated it as one of the specific acts of the Lord, for the most destiny-shaping issue in human life, and at the same time the most complex issue in the universe and the clearest proof of Allah's power, is this very issue of life and death.

Key Point: The Relationship Between the Quran and the Night of Decree

It is noteworthy that in the above verses by implication, and in the verses of Surah al-Qadr explicitly, this meaning is conveyed: that the Quran was revealed on the Night of Decree. How meaningful is this statement?

The night in which the destinies of the servants and their blessings and provisions are ordained—on such a night, the Quran is revealed to the pure heart of the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family). Does this statement not mean that your destinies and fate are closely linked and related to the content of this Heavenly Book?! Does the concept of this speech not mean that not only your spiritual life, but also your material life, has an inseparable relationship with it? Your victory over enemies, your honor, freedom, and independence, the prosperity and development of your cities—all are tied to it.

Yes, on that night when destinies were being determined, the Quran was also revealed on that night!

[^1]: The word "Rabb" (Lord) in this verse is an appositive (badal) for "Rabb" in the previous verse.

[^2]: The apodosis (jaza') of the conditional sentence "in kuntum muqinin" is omitted, and is estimated as: "If you are among the people of certainty or in search of certainty, you would know that Allah is the Lord of the heavens and the earth and what is between them."

[^1]: The phrase "la ilaha illa hu" may be an introductory sentence (istinafiyah) or the predicate of an omitted subject, estimated as "Huwa la ilaha illa hu," but the first possibility is more appropriate.

Ayahs

بَلْ هُمْ فِى شَكٍّ يَلْعَبُونَ (٩)
But they play around in doubt. (9)
فَٱرْتَقِبْ يَوْمَ تَأْتِى ٱلسَّمَآءُ بِدُخَانٍ مُّبِينٍ (١٠)
So watch out for the day when the sky brings on a manifest smoke, (10)
يَغْشَى ٱلنَّاسَ ۖ هَـٰذَا عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (١١)
enveloping the people. [They will cry out:] ‘This is a painful punishment. (11)
رَّبَّنَا ٱكْشِفْ عَنَّا ٱلْعَذَابَ إِنَّا مُؤْمِنُونَ (١٢)
Our Lord! Remove this punishment from us. Indeed we have believed!’ (12)
أَنَّىٰ لَهُمُ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ وَقَدْ جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مُّبِينٌ (١٣)
What will the admonition avail them, when a manifest apostle had already come to them, (13)
ثُمَّ تَوَلَّوْا۟ عَنْهُ وَقَالُوا۟ مُعَلَّمٌ مَّجْنُونٌ (١٤)
but they turned away from him, and said, ‘A tutored madman?’ (14)
إِنَّا كَاشِفُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابِ قَلِيلًا ۚ إِنَّكُمْ عَآئِدُونَ (١٥)
Indeed We will withdraw the punishment a little; but you will revert [to your earlier ways]. (15)
يَوْمَ نَبْطِشُ ٱلْبَطْشَةَ ٱلْكُبْرَىٰٓ إِنَّا مُنتَقِمُونَ (١٦)
The day We shall strike with the most terrible striking, We will indeed take vengeance [on them]. (16)

Commentary

The Day a Lethal Smoke Envelopes the Entire Sky

Since the previous verses mentioned that if they were seekers of certainty, the means to attain it are abundant and available, the first verse under discussion adds: They are not seeking certainty or the truth, "But they are in doubt, playing" (بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ يَلْعَبُونَ).

If they doubt the authenticity of this Heavenly Book and your prophethood, it is not because the matter is complex; rather, it is because they do not take it seriously and approach it with jest. Sometimes they mock and ridicule, and sometimes they feign ignorance and unawareness, distracting themselves every day with a different kind of "play."

"Yal‘abun" (playing) is derived from the root "lu‘ab," which according to Raghib means saliva when it flows. Since a person has no significant goal in their actions during play and jest, it is likened to saliva that flows involuntarily from a person's mouth.

In any case, it is a reality that a serious approach to issues helps a person recognize realities, while non-serious encounters cast a veil over them.

In the next verse, the text turns to threaten these stubborn and persistent deniers while addressing the Prophet, saying: "Then watch for the Day when the sky will bring a visible smoke" (فارْتَقِبْ يَوْمَ تَأْتِي السَّماءُ بِدُخانٍ مُبِينٍ).

"Enveloping the people" (يغشى الناس). "And then it will be said to them: This is a painful torment!" (هذا عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ).

Terror and anxiety will overwhelm their entire being; the veils of negligence will be removed from before their eyes, and realizing their great mistake, they will turn to the threshold of Allah and say: "Our Lord, remove the torment from us; indeed, we are believers" (رَبَّنَا اكْشِفْ عَنَّا الْعَذابَ إِنَّا مُؤْمِنُونَ).

But He rejects these strangers and says: "How can there be for them a reminder [at that time], when there had already come to them a clear Messenger with miracles and evident proofs?" (أَنَّى لَهُمُ الذِّكْرى‌ وَ قَدْ جاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ مُبِينٌ). A Messenger who was himself manifest, and whose teachings, programs, proofs, and miracles were all clear and evident.

But instead of submitting to his command, believing in the One God, and accepting His instructions with their souls, "They turned away from him and said: [He is] one taught [by others], a madman!" (ثُمَّ تَوَلَّوْا عَنْهُ وَ قالُوا مُعَلَّمٌ مَجْنُونٌ).

Sometimes they said a "Roman slave" who had heard the stories of the prophets was teaching him, and that these verses were his fabrication! وَ لَقَدْ نَعْلَمُ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ، إِنَّما يُعَلِّمُهُ بَشَرٌ لِسانُ الَّذِي يُلْحِدُونَ إِلَيْهِ أَعْجَمِيٌّ وَ هذا لِسانٌ عَرَبِيٌّ مُبِينٌ: "And We certainly know that they say, 'It is only a human being who teaches him.' The tongue of the one they refer to is foreign, while this Qur'an is a clear Arabic tongue" (Nahl - 103).

And sometimes they said he had suffered a mental breakdown, and these words were the result of losing his intellectual balance.

Then He adds: "Indeed, We will remove the torment a little, but you will return [to your ways]" (إِنَّا كاشِفُوا الْعَذابِ قَلِيلًا إِنَّكُمْ عائِدُونَ). From here it becomes clear that if they feel regret for their deeds and decide to reconsider while in the grip of torment, it is temporary and fleeting. As soon as the storm of events subsides, the previous patterns are repeated.

And in the final verse under discussion, He says: "The Day We will strike with the greatest strike; indeed, We will take retribution" (يَوْمَ نَبْطِشُ الْبَطْشَةَ الْكُبْرى‌ إِنَّا مُنْتَقِمُونَ) [1].

[1]: Many possibilities have been suggested regarding the syntax of this sentence. What is most accepted by commentators and fits the tone of the verse is that "Yawm" (the Day) relates to the verb "Nantaqim" (We take retribution) understood from the phrase "Inna Muntaqimun." Thus, the implied meaning is: "We shall take retribution from them on the Day We strike with the greatest strike; indeed, We are the ones who take retribution."

"Batsh" (rhyming with Naqsh) means seizing something with power, and here it means seizing for severe punishment. Describing "Batshah" as "Kubra" (Greatest) refers to the hard and heavy punishment awaiting this group.

In short, even if their periodic punishments are lightened or ceased, the severe and final punishment awaits them, and they have no way to escape it.

"Muntaqimun" is from the root "Intiqam," which, as we have said before, means to punish. Although the word "Intiqam" (revenge) in current everyday usage conveys a different meaning associated with quenching the fire of anger and heartache, these elements do not exist in its literal linguistic meaning.

Key Point: What is meant by "Visible Smoke"?

There is a discussion among commentators regarding what is meant by the "Dukhan" (smoke) mentioned in these verses as a sign of divine punishment. There are two main theories here:

1- It refers to a punishment that the Quraysh disbelievers were afflicted with during the time of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, because the Prophet prayed against them, saying:

اللهم سنين كسنى يوسف!

"O Allah, bring upon them years of famine and drought like the years of the time of Joseph!"

After this event, such a famine occurred in the environment of Mecca that due to hunger and thirst, when they looked at the sky, it was as if there was smoke and haze between them and the sky. Matters became so difficult for them that they ate carcasses and the bones of dead animals.

They came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "O Muhammad! You command us to maintain family ties, while your relatives have perished in this event. If this torment is removed from us, we will believe."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله prayed for them; abundance and blessings were granted to them, and the calamity and torment were removed. However, they did not take a lesson from this event and returned to disbelief [1]. According to this interpretation, "The Greatest Strike" (al-Batshah al-Kubra), which is the severe punishment, is taken to mean the event of the Battle of Badr, where this group of polytheists received heavy and crushing blows from the Muslims.

According to this interpretation, no actual smoke existed; rather, the sky appeared dark and hazy in the eyes of the hungry and thirsty people. Therefore, the mention of "Smoke" here is metaphorical, referring to that difficult and painful state.

Some have said that in Arabic literature, "Dukhan" is fundamentally an allusion to widespread evil and calamity [2]. Others believe that during droughts and lack of rain, dark dust usually covers the face of the sky, which is referred to here as "Smoke," because it is rain that settles the dust and clears the air [3]. Despite all these descriptions, the mention of the word "Smoke" according to this interpretation would be metaphorical.

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 62 (under the verses discussed).

[2]: Fakhr al-Razi says: "The Arabs call overwhelming evil 'Dukhan'" (Vol. 27, p. 242).

[3]: "Ruh al-Ma'ani," Vol. 25, p. 107.

2- "Visible Smoke" refers to the thick smoke that will cover the sky at the end of the world and on the threshold of the Resurrection. it is a sign of the arrival of the final moments of the world and the beginning of the painful divine torment for the oppressors and the corrupt.

At this point, this group of wrongdoers will wake up from their sleep of negligence and request the removal of the torment and a return to normal worldly life, but they will be rejected.

According to this interpretation, "Smoke" carries its literal meaning, and the content of these verses is the same as what appears in other verses of the Qur'an: that sinners and disbelievers on the threshold of the Resurrection or during the Resurrection itself will request the removal of torment and a return to the world, but it will not be accepted from them [1]. The only problem this interpretation faces is that it does not seem to align with the sentence: إِنَّا كاشِفُوا الْعَذابِ قَلِيلًا إِنَّكُمْ عائِدُونَ: "Indeed, We will remove the torment a little, but you will return [to your ways]," because at the end of the world or in the Resurrection, the divine punishment is not lightened for people to return to a state of disbelief and sin.

However, if we interpret this sentence as a conditional proposition—though it is slightly contrary to the literal appearance—the problem is resolved. For the meaning of the verse would be: "Whenever We remove the torment from them a little, they take up their former path again," which is in fact similar to verse 28 of Surah Al-An'am: وَ لَوْ رُدُّوا لَعادُوا لِما نُهُوا عَنْهُ: "And if they were returned, they would certainly revert to that which they were forbidden."

Furthermore, interpreting "The Greatest Strike" (al-Batshah al-Kubra) as the events of the day of Badr seems unlikely, whereas it is perfectly consistent with the punishments of the Resurrection [1]. Another piece of evidence for the second interpretation is the narrations quoted from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله himself, who interpreted "Dukhan" as the smoke that will envelop the world on the threshold of the Resurrection. For example, the narration that Hudhayfah ibn al-Yaman quotes from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, where he mentioned four things as signs of the approaching Resurrection: first, the appearance of the Dajjal; second, the descent of Jesus; third, a fire that rises from the depths of the land of "Aden"; and "Smoke."

Hudhayfah asks: "O Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), what is the Smoke?" Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) recited:

فَارْتَقِبْ يَوْمَ تَأْتِي السَّماءُ بِدُخانٍ مُبِينٍ

"It is a smoke that fills what is between the East and the West; it remains for forty days and nights. As for the believer, he is affected by it like a cold (catarrh), but as for the disbeliever, he becomes like a drunken man; it comes out of his nostrils, his ears, and his rear." [2] In another Hadith from Abu Malik al-Ash'ari from the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, it is stated:

"Indeed, your Lord has warned you of three things: the Smoke, which affects the believer like a cold and affects the disbeliever such that he swells until it comes out of every orifice of his; second, the Beast (Dabbat al-Ard); and third, the Dajjal." [3]

[1]: Refer to verses 27-30 of Surah Al-An'am.

[1]: Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Al-Batsh is seizing a thing with force," which is usually a prelude to punishment.

[2] and [3]: Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 29.

Regarding "Dabbat al-Ard," a sufficient explanation was given in Vol. 15, p. 551, under verse 82 of Surah An-Naml.

A similar meaning regarding "Dukhan" has been narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri from the Holy Prophet [1]. In narrations transmitted through the channels of the Ahl al-Bayt عليهم السلام, similar expressions—and even more detailed ones—are seen. Among them, in a narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, we read that he said the Messenger of Allah said:

"There are ten signs before the Hour that must occur: the Sufyani, the Dajjal, the Smoke, the Beast, the emergence of the Qa'im (عليه السلام), the rising of the sun from the west, the descent of Jesus, a landslide in the East, a landslide in the Arabian Peninsula, and a fire that emerges from the depths of Aden, driving the people to the place of Gathering." [2]

From the sum of what has been said, we conclude that the second interpretation is more appropriate.

[1]: Same source.

[2]: "Bihar al-Anwar," Vol. 52, p. 209.

Ayahs

۞ وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَجَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ كَرِيمٌ (١٧)
Certainly We tried the people of Pharaoh before them, when a noble apostle came to them, (17)
أَنْ أَدُّوٓا۟ إِلَىَّ عِبَادَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ (١٨)
[saying,] ‘Give over the servants of Allah to me; indeed I am a trusted apostle [sent] to you. (18)
وَأَن لَّا تَعْلُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنِّىٓ ءَاتِيكُم بِسُلْطَـٰنٍ مُّبِينٍ (١٩)
Do not defy Allah. Indeed I bring you a manifest authority. (19)
وَإِنِّى عُذْتُ بِرَبِّى وَرَبِّكُمْ أَن تَرْجُمُونِ (٢٠)
I seek the protection of my Lord and your Lord, lest you should stone me. (20)
وَإِن لَّمْ تُؤْمِنُوا۟ لِى فَٱعْتَزِلُونِ (٢١)
And if you do not believe me, keep out of my way.’ (21)

Commentary

If You Do Not Believe, at Least Do Not Obstruct Others

Following the previous verses which spoke of the rebellion of the Arab polytheists and their refusal to submit to the Truth, the verses under discussion point to an example from previous nations who followed the same path and ultimately suffered a painful punishment and a manifest defeat. This serves both as a consolation for the believers and a warning for the stubborn deniers.

It is the story of Moses and Pharaoh; He says: "And We certainly tried the people of Pharaoh before them" (وَلَقَدْ فَتَنَّا قَبْلَهُمْ قَوْمَ فِرْعَوْنَ).

"Fatanna" is derived from the root "fitnah," which originally means placing gold in a furnace to purify it from impurities. Subsequently, it has been applied to any kind of trial and examination conducted to test the degree of human sincerity—a trial that encompasses the entirety of the lives of individuals and human societies. In other words, the entire lifespan of a human in this world is spent in these trials, as this world is the abode of testing.

The people of Pharaoh lived at the height of power, possessing a mighty government, abundant wealth, and vast resources. This great power made them arrogant, and they became tainted with various types of sin, injustice, and oppression.

"And there came to them a noble messenger" (وَجَاءَهُمْ رَسُولٌ كَرِيمٌ).

"Karim" (noble/generous) in terms of character and temperament, "Karim" in terms of value and greatness in the presence of the Truth, and "Karim" in terms of lineage and origin. This messenger was none other than Moses son of Amram عليه السلام. [^1]

Moses addressed them in a very polite, pleasant tone, full of affection, and said: "The purpose of my coming is that you, O servants of Allah, submit to me, for I am His messenger, and fulfill what He has commanded you" (أَنْ أَدُّوا إِلَيَّ عِبَادَ اللَّهِ). ^2 According to this interpretation, "servants of Allah" (عباد الله) refers to the audience, meaning the Pharaohs. Although this expression is used in Quranic verses for righteous servants, it has also been applied in several instances to disbelievers and sinners to comfort them and attract their hearts toward the Truth. [^3] Therefore, the meaning of "addu" (fulfill/render) is to obey Allah's command and carry out His instructions.

A group of commentators has mentioned another interpretation for this sentence. They said that "servants of Allah" refers to the Children of Israel, and "addu" means handing them over to Moses and ending their captivity and slavery, similar to what appears in verse 17 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara: "That you send with us the Children of Israel" (أَنْ أَرْسِلْ مَعَنَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ). (A similar meaning also appears in Al-A'raf 105 and Taha 47).

The point that does not align with this interpretation is the word "addu," which is usually used for rendering property, trusts, and duties, not for the handing over of persons (this becomes clearly evident by observing the usage cases of this term).

In any case, in the continuation of the verse, to negate any accusation against himself, he adds: "Indeed, I am to you a trustworthy messenger" (إِنِّي لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ).

This expression is, in fact, a preemptive measure against the false attributions the Pharaohs made against him, such as the accusation of sorcery, the intent to seek superiority and rule over the land of Egypt, and the expulsion of the original owners of that land, which are mentioned in various verses.

Then Moses عليه السلام, after inviting them to obey Allah or free the Children of Israel, says: My other mission is to tell you: "And do not exalt yourselves against Allah. Indeed, I come to you with a clear authority" (وَأَنْ لَا تَعْلُوا عَلَى اللَّهِ إِنِّي آتِيكُمْ بِسُلْطَانٍ مُبِينٍ). Both manifest miracles and clear logical proofs.

The meaning of "not exalting oneself" against Allah refers to any action inconsistent with the principles of servitude, ranging from opposition and disobedience to harassing the messengers of the Lord, or claiming divinity and lordship and the like.

And since the world-worshipping arrogant ones do not refrain from any kind of accusation, insult, or even murder and execution against anyone they see opposing their illegitimate interests, Moses عليه السلام adds as a precaution: "And indeed, I have sought refuge in my Lord and your Lord, lest you stone me" (وَإِنِّي عُذْتُ بِرَبِّي وَرَبِّكُمْ أَنْ تَرْجُمُونِ).

This expression may indicate that "I do not fear your threats, and I stand firm until the last breath, and Allah is my Protector and Guardian." Such expressions gave more decisiveness to the call of divine leaders, weakened the will of the enemies, and increased the steadfastness of friends, for they knew their leader would resist until the final stage.

The emphasis on the issue of "rajm" (stoning) might stem from the fact that many divine messengers before Moses عليه السلام were threatened with "rajm," including Noah: "They said, 'If you do not desist, O Noah, you will surely be of those who are stoned'" (Ash-Shu'ara: 116).

And Abraham عليه السلام, where Azar threatened him and said: "If you do not desist, I will surely stone you" (Maryam: 46). And Shu'ayb, where the idolaters threatened him: "And if not for your family, we would have stoned you" (Hud: 91).

The choice of stoning among types of killing is because it is the harshest. According to some lexicographers, this word has also been used to mean killing in general. [^1] This possibility has also been suggested by many commentators that "rajm" means accusing and reviling, as the word is used in this sense as well, and it is actually a precaution against the accusations they later leveled against Moses.

The use of this word in a broad sense that includes both meanings is also possible.

In the last verse under discussion, he says his final word to them: "But if you do not believe me, then leave me alone" (وَإِنْ لَمْ تُؤْمِنُوا لِي فَاعْتَزِلُونِ). For Moses عليه السلام was confident that, possessing those brilliant miracles, strong proofs, manifest authority, and divine promises, he would continue his progress among various classes of people, and his revolution would soon bear fruit. Therefore, he was content if they would just not block his path and not interfere with him.

But is it possible for the arrogant tyrants, who see their satanic power and illegitimate interests in danger, to remain silent and accept such a proposal? The coming verses will explain the continuation of this event.

[^1]: According to "Al-Mufradat": "Karim," when used as a description of Allah, means His manifest benevolence and bounty. When a human is described by it, it refers to the worthy character and actions that appear from him (of course, this description appears in the Holy Quran for other things as well, such as a noble book, every noble kind, noble provision, a noble position, and a noble reward).

[^3]: Like Al-Furqan: 17, Saba: 13, Al-Furqan: 58, and others.

[^1]: "Lisan al-Arab," root "rajm."

Ayahs

فَدَعَا رَبَّهُۥٓ أَنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ قَوْمٌ مُّجْرِمُونَ (٢٢)
Then he invoked his Lord, [saying,] ‘These are indeed a guilty lot.’ (22)
فَأَسْرِ بِعِبَادِى لَيْلًا إِنَّكُم مُّتَّبَعُونَ (٢٣)
[Allah told him,] ‘Set out by night with My servants; for you will be pursued. (23)
وَٱتْرُكِ ٱلْبَحْرَ رَهْوًا ۖ إِنَّهُمْ جُندٌ مُّغْرَقُونَ (٢٤)
Leave behind the sea unmoving; for they will be a drowned host.’ (24)
كَمْ تَرَكُوا۟ مِن جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (٢٥)
How many gardens and springs did they leave behind! (25)
وَزُرُوعٍ وَمَقَامٍ كَرِيمٍ (٢٦)
Fields and splendid places, (26)
وَنَعْمَةٍ كَانُوا۟ فِيهَا فَـٰكِهِينَ (٢٧)
and the affluence wherein they rejoiced! (27)
كَذَٰلِكَ ۖ وَأَوْرَثْنَـٰهَا قَوْمًا ءَاخَرِينَ (٢٨)
So it was; and We bequeathed them to another people. (28)
فَمَا بَكَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلسَّمَآءُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ مُنظَرِينَ (٢٩)
So neither the sky wept for them, nor the earth; nor were they granted any respite. (29)

Commentary

They Left Behind Palaces, Gardens, and Treasures!

Musa عليه السلام utilized every means of guidance to penetrate the dark hearts of these criminals, but it had no effect on the Pharaohs. He knocked on every possible door, but "ultimately, no head emerged from that door!" Consequently, he became despaired and saw no remedy but to invoke a curse upon them. This is because a corrupt people, for whose guidance there is no hope, have no right to life according to the system of creation; Divine punishment must descend to harvest them and cleanse the face of the earth from the filth of their existence.

Therefore, the first verse under discussion says: "Musa called upon his Lord, saying: 'These are indeed a criminal and sinful people'" (فَدَعا رَبَّهُ أَنَّ هؤُلاءِ قَوْمٌ مُجْرِمُونَ‌).

What a polite invocation! He does not say, "O Allah, do such and such to them," but rather simply says: "O Allah, these are a group of criminals for whose guidance no hope remains."

Allah accepted his prayer and, as a prelude to the descent of punishment upon the Pharaohs and the salvation of the Children of Israel, He commanded Musa عليه السلام: "Depart with My servants by night, for Pharaoh and his army will surely pursue you" (فَأَسْرِ بِعِبادِي لَيْلًا إِنَّكُمْ مُتَّبَعُونَ‌).

But do not worry; it is necessary for them to pursue you so that they may meet the fate that awaits them.

Musa عليه السلام was commissioned to move the believing servants of God—namely the Children of Israel who had believed in him, along with a group of Egyptians whose hearts were more receptive and had responded to his call—by night. They were to head toward the banks of the Nile, cross it miraculously, and set out for the Promised Land, Palestine.

It is true that the movement of Musa and his followers took place at night, but certainly, the movement of such a vast population could not remain hidden from the Pharaohs for long. Perhaps no more than a few hours had passed before Pharaoh's spies brought him news of this great event—the mass escape of the slaves, so to speak. Pharaoh ordered a massive army to pursue them. Interestingly, all these details are conveyed with a short and concise allusion in the aforementioned verses: "Indeed, you will be pursued" (انكم متبعون).

What has been omitted here for the sake of brevity is expressed in short phrases in other verses of the Quran. As we read in Surah Ta-Ha, verse 77: وَ لَقَدْ أَوْحَيْنا إِلى‌ مُوسى‌ أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبادِي فَاضْرِبْ لَهُمْ طَرِيقاً فِي الْبَحْرِ يَبَساً لا تَخافُ دَرَكاً وَ لا تَخْشى‌: "We inspired Musa, saying: 'Take My servants away by night, and strike for them a dry path through the sea, fearing neither being overtaken [by Pharaoh] nor dreading [drowning]!'"

Then, in the verses under discussion, it adds: "And when you have all crossed the sea safely, leave the sea at rest and wide open" (وَ اتْرُكِ الْبَحْرَ رَهْواً).

The "sea" referred to in these verses is the great Nile River.

Commentators and lexicographers have mentioned two meanings for "Rahw" (rhyming with Sahw): "being at rest/calm" and "being wide open." There is no obstacle to combining both meanings here.

But why was such a command given to Musa عليه السلام? Naturally, Musa عليه السلام and the Children of Israel would have preferred that once they crossed the sea, the waters would crash back together and close this massive gap so they could distance themselves quickly and safely toward the Promised Land. However, they were commanded not to hurry when crossing the Nile; leave it open so that Pharaoh and his soldiers enter to the last man, for the command of death and destruction has been issued to the roaring waves of the Nile!

Thus, at the end of the verse, it adds: "They are an army that shall be drowned" (إِنَّهُمْ جُنْدٌ مُغْرَقُونَ‌).

This is the definitive decree of Allah regarding this arrogant and rebellious group: they must all be buried in that very Nile River from which all their wealth and power originated. Their source of life, by a Divine command, shall turn into their cause of death.

Indeed, when Pharaoh and his army reached the banks of the Nile, the Children of Israel had emerged from the other side. Although the appearance of such a path in the middle of the Nile was enough to make even a child realize a great Divine miracle had occurred, pride and arrogance did not allow those stubborn ones to perceive this clear reality, to realize their mistakes, and turn toward God. Perhaps they imagined that the transformation of the Nile was also by Pharaoh's command! Perhaps he said the same to his followers and personally entered the path, and his followers followed him to the last man. But suddenly, the waves of the Nile collapsed like a dilapidated building whose pillars have broken, and they were all drowned.

A point that clearly draws attention in these verses is their extraordinary brevity despite being highly expressive. By omitting additional sentences that can be understood from context or other verses, a detailed story is recounted in three verses or three short sentences. It simply says: "Musa appealed to his Lord that these are criminals; he was told to move My servants by night and that you will be pursued; leave the sea wide and calm, for they are a drowned army!" The expression "drowned" (مُغْرَقُونَ‌), even though they had not yet drowned, refers to the certainty and inevitability of this Divine decree.

Now let us see what exemplary events took place after the drowning of Pharaoh and his people. In the following verses, the Quran describes their vast heritage that passed to the Children of Israel through five subjects that summarize their entire lifestyle:

First, He says: "How many gardens and springs they left behind!" (كَمْ تَرَكُوا مِنْ جَنَّاتٍ وَ عُيُونٍ‌).

Gardens and springs were two of their most attractive and valuable assets, for Egypt, thanks to the Nile, was a fertile land full of gardens. These "springs" might refer to natural springs flowing from the foothills of mountains or branches originating from the Nile that passed through their lush and green gardens. Applying the term "spring" (Ayn) to these branches is not far-fetched.

Then it adds: "And crops, and noble and beautiful dwellings" (وَ زُرُوعٍ وَ مَقامٍ كَرِيمٍ‌).

These were two other important assets of theirs: the vast crops grown in the basin of the Nile throughout Egypt—various types of food and non-food products which they used themselves and exported abroad, and upon which their economy revolved—and also the palaces and inhabited dwellings, as suitable housing is one of the most important means of human life.

Of course, the "nobility" (Karim) and high value of these palaces were from an outward perspective and in their own eyes; otherwise, in the logic of the Quran, such glittering, tyrannical, and negligence-inducing dwellings possess no true nobility.

Some have also suggested that "Maqam Karim" refers to festive gatherings and celebrations, or the pulpits upon which praisers and poets stood to eulogize Pharaoh, but the first meaning seems most appropriate.

And since they had many other means of luxury besides the four major items mentioned above, He refers to all of them in one short sentence, saying: "And [other] comforts wherein they used to take delight" (وَ نَعْمَةٍ كانُوا فِيها فاكِهِينَ‌). 1

[1]: "Na'mah" (with a Fatha on the Nun) means "luxury/delight," and with a Kasra on the Nun, it means "favor/blessing." A group of commentators and lexicographers have specified this meaning, while others believe both have the same meaning and include any significant benefit.

2: "Fakihin" is sometimes interpreted as enjoying "Fawakih" (fruits), sometimes as engaging in humorous and joyful conversation, and sometimes as any kind of luxury and pleasure. The latter meaning is the most comprehensive.

Then He adds: Indeed, "Thus it was, and We made another people inherit them" (كَذلِكَ وَ أَوْرَثْناها قَوْماً آخَرِينَ‌). [1] The "other people" refers to the Children of Israel, as explicitly stated in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verse 59. The term "inheritance" (Irth) indicates that they acquired all these properties and riches without trouble or heartache, just as a person gains an inheritance without toil.

It is noteworthy that the above verse and its counterpart in Surah Ash-Shu'ara indicate that the Children of Israel returned to the land of Egypt after the drowning of the Pharaohs, inherited the legacy of the Pharaohs, and ruled there. The course of events also necessitates this, as after the collapse of the pillars of Pharaonic power in Egypt, Musa عليه السلام would never have allowed that country to suffer a political vacuum.

However, this does not contradict what is mentioned in other Quranic verses—that after being saved from Pharaoh, the Children of Israel moved toward the Promised Land of Palestine, the events of which are detailed in the Quran. It is possible that a group of them remained in the land of Egypt, which had fallen into their hands, as representatives of Musa عليه السلام, while the majority headed toward Palestine.

(For further explanation on this, see Volume 15, page 239, under verse 59 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara).

[1]: "Kadhalika" (Thus) is the predicate of an omitted subject, implied as "The matter was thus." This expression is used for emphasis. Some other grammatical possibilities have also been suggested.

In the final verse under discussion, He says: "Neither the heaven nor the earth wept for them, nor were they given any respite [when the calamity descended]!" (فَما بَكَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ السَّماءُ وَ الْأَرْضُ وَ ما كانُوا مُنْظَرِينَ‌).

The heaven and earth not weeping for them may be a metaphor for their insignificance and the absence of any friend, helper, or sympathizer for them, as is common in Arabic usage. When people want to express the importance of someone who has suffered a calamity, they say: "The heaven and earth wept for him, and the sun and moon darkened for his loss."

It has also been suggested that it means "the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth did not weep," for they weep for the believers and those close to Allah, not for tyrants like the Pharaohs.

Some have also said: The weeping of the heaven and earth is a literal weeping that manifests as a type of transformation and a specific redness (in addition to the usual redness at sunrise and sunset).

As we read in a narration: "When Al-Husayn ibn Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه السلام was killed, the heaven wept for him, and its weeping was the redness of its horizons." [1]

In another narration from Imam Al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "The heaven wept for Yahya ibn Zakariyya (who was martyred in a very heart-wrenching way by the tyrant of his time) and for Al-Husayn ibn Ali (peace be upon them both) for forty mornings, and it did not weep for anyone except those two. The narrator asked: 'What was the weeping of the heaven?' He replied: 'It would rise red and set red.'" [1]

However, in a Hadith narrated from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "There is no believer except that he has a gate in heaven through which his deeds ascend, and a gate from which his provision descends; when he dies, these two gates weep for him." 2

There is no contradiction between these narrations, because in the case of the martyrdom of Imam Al-Husayn عليه السلام and Yahya ibn Zakariyya عليه السلام, the matter had a general aspect across the entire sky, whereas what is mentioned in the latter narration has a localized aspect. [3] In any case, there is no conflict between these interpretations, and they can all be included in the concept of the verse.

Indeed, for the death of "evildoers," neither "the eye of the firmament wept" nor "the heart of the sun became withered." They were foul beings who seemingly had no connection to the world of existence or the world of humanity. When these strangers were expelled from the world, no one felt their absence—not on the stage of the earth, nor across the expanse of the heaven, nor in the depths of human hearts. For this reason, no one shed a single tear for their death.

We conclude the discussion on these verses by quoting a narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام:

It is narrated that when the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, passed by Madain and observed the ruins of Khosrow (Anushirvan and the Sassanid kings) which were near collapse, one of those in his service recited this poem as a lesson: "The winds blew over the traces of their dwellings—as if they all had an appointment [with death] and hurried toward it!"

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, said: "Why did you not recite this verse: 'How many gardens and springs they left behind, and crops and noble dwellings, and comforts wherein they used to take delight... Neither the heaven nor the earth wept for them, nor were they given any respite'?" [1]

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 65 (under the verses discussed).

[3]: In Al-Durr al-Manthur, a Hadith is also narrated regarding the reconciliation of these narrations (as cited in Al-Mizan, Vol. 18, p. 151). [1]: Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, p. 531 (under the root m-d-n).

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ نَجَّيْنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْمُهِينِ (٣٠)
Certainly We delivered the Children of Israel from the humiliating torment (30)
مِن فِرْعَوْنَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ عَالِيًا مِّنَ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (٣١)
of Pharaoh. Indeed, he was a tyrant among the transgressors. (31)
وَلَقَدِ ٱخْتَرْنَـٰهُمْ عَلَىٰ عِلْمٍ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٣٢)
Certainly We chose them knowingly above all the nations, (32)
وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُم مِّنَ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ مَا فِيهِ بَلَـٰٓؤٌا۟ مُّبِينٌ (٣٣)
and We gave them some signs in which there was a manifest test. (33)

Commentary

The Children of Israel in the Crucible of Trial

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the drowning and destruction of the Pharaoh’s people, the annihilation of their power and glory, and its transfer to others. The verses under discussion speak of the opposite side of that event—namely, the salvation and liberation of the Children of Israel—as follows:

"And We certainly delivered the Children of Israel from the humiliating punishment" (وَ لَقَدْ نَجَّيْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ مِنَ الْعَذابِ الْمُهِينِ‌).

From the severe and grueling physical and psychological tortures that penetrated to the depths of their souls: from the slaughtering of their newborn sons and the sparing of their daughters for servitude and lust, from forced labor and extremely heavy tasks, and the like. How painful is the fate of a nation and a people caught in the clutches of such a bloodthirsty and demonic enemy.

Yes, Allah delivered this oppressed people from the clutches of these bloodthirsty tyrants of history in the light of the divine uprising of Musa ibn 'Imran عليه السلام; therefore, He adds immediately after: "From Pharaoh" (مِنْ فِرْعَوْنَ‌).

"Indeed, he was haughty and among the transgressors [and the prodigals]!" (إِنَّهُ كانَ عالِياً مِنَ الْمُسْرِفِينَ‌).

The meaning of "‘Ali" (haughty/high) here does not refer to high status, but rather points to his seeking of superiority and his excess in extravagance and transgression, just as it appears in Surah Al-Qasas, verse 4: "Indeed, Pharaoh exalted himself in the land" (إِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ عَلا فِي الْأَرْضِ‌)—to the extent that he even claimed divinity and called himself the Most High Lord.

"Musrif" is derived from the root "Israf," meaning any form of exceeding the limit in actions and speech. For this reason, the word "Musrif" is used in various verses of the Quran regarding criminals who exceeded the limits of oppression and corruption. Furthermore, absolute sins are also referred to as Israf, as we read in Surah Az-Zumar, verse 53: "Say, 'O My servants who have transgressed against themselves [by sinning], do not despair of the mercy of Allah'" (قُلْ يا عِبادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلى‌ أَنْفُسِهِمْ لا تَقْنَطُوا مِنْ رَحْمَةِ اللَّهِ‌).

In the next verse, referring to another of Allah's blessings upon the Children of Israel, He says: "And We certainly chose them based on knowledge over the worlds [of their time]" (وَ لَقَدِ اخْتَرْناهُمْ عَلى‌ عِلْمٍ عَلَى الْعالَمِينَ‌).

However, they did not appreciate these blessings, acted with ingratitude, and were subsequently punished.

Thus, they were the "chosen nation of their era," because "the worlds" (al-'Alamin) refers to the people of the world during that specific age and time, not across all centuries and ages. This is because the Quran explicitly addresses the Islamic Ummah in Surah Al-Imran, verse 110, saying: "You are the best nation produced [as an example] for mankind..." (كُنْتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ‌).

Similarly, regarding the lands that the Children of Israel inherited, He says in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 137: "And We caused the people who had been oppressed to inherit the eastern and western parts of the land which We had blessed" (وَ أَوْرَثْنَا الْقَوْمَ الَّذِينَ كانُوا يُسْتَضْعَفُونَ مَشارِقَ الْأَرْضِ وَ مَغارِبَهَا الَّتِي بارَكْنا فِيها).

It is clear that the Children of Israel did not become the heirs of the entire world at that time; rather, it refers to the east and west of their own region.

Of course, some commentators believe that the Children of Israel possessed certain advantages that were unique to them throughout history, including the abundance of prophets, as no other nation has seen so many prophets rise from among them.

However, this point, besides not proving their absolute superiority, may not necessarily be an advantage; for it is possible to consider the frequent rising of prophets among them as evidence of the extreme rebelliousness and defiance of this people. As various events after the uprising of Musa عليه السلام show, what did they not do to this great prophet?!

In any case, what we have provided above in the commentary of the verse is what has been accepted by many commentators regarding the relative merit of the Children of Israel.

However, considering that this stubborn group—according to the Quran—constantly harassed their prophets and stood against divine decrees with particular persistence and prejudice, and even when they had just been delivered from the Nile, they suggested idol-making to Musa, it could be said that the above verse is not stating a merit but rather expressing another reality. The meaning of the verse would then be: "Despite Our knowledge that they would misuse divine blessings, We gave them superiority in order to test them," just as it can be inferred from the next verse that Allah gave them other blessings to test them.

Thus, this divine selection is not only not a proof of their merit, but rather a subtle reproach is embedded within it, for they did not fulfill the rights of this blessing and did not pass the test.

In the final verse under discussion, referring to some other blessings Allah had bestowed upon them, He says: "And We gave them signs in which there was a clear trial" (وَ آتَيْناهُمْ مِنَ الْآياتِ ما فِيهِ بَلؤُا مُبِينٌ‌).

Sometimes in the wilderness of "Sinai" and the valley of "Tih," We made the clouds cast shade over them; sometimes We sent down the special food of "Manna and Quail" upon them; sometimes We caused springs of water to flow for them from the heart of hard rock; and at other times, We granted them other spiritual and material blessings.

But all of these were for the purpose of trial and testing, for Allah tests one group with calamity and another with blessing, as we read in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 168: "And We tested them with good [times] and bad [times] that perhaps they would return [to obedience]" (وَ بَلَوْناهُمْ بِالْحَسَناتِ وَ السَّيِّئاتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ‌).

Perhaps the purpose of mentioning this history of the Children of Israel to the early Muslims is so that they would not fear the multitude of enemies and their great power, and be certain that the God who crushed the powerful Pharaohs and made the Children of Israel the heirs to their kingdom and government would grant you such a victory in the not-too-distant future. However, just as they were tested with these blessings, you too will be placed severely in the furnace of trial to see what you will do after gaining power and victory. This is a warning to all nations and peoples regarding the victories and blessings they obtain through divine grace: that the snare of trial is most difficult at such times.

Ayahs

إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ لَيَقُولُونَ (٣٤)
These ones say, (34)
إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّا مَوْتَتُنَا ٱلْأُولَىٰ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُنشَرِينَ (٣٥)
‘It will be only our first death, and we shall not be resurrected. (35)
فَأْتُوا۟ بِـَٔابَآئِنَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٣٦)
Bring our fathers back [to life], if you are truthful.’ (36)

Commentary

There is Nothing But This Death!

After depicting a scene from the life of Pharaoh and his people and the consequence of their disbelief and denial in the previous verses, the discourse once again turns to the polytheists. It recounts their doubt regarding the issue of Resurrection—which was mentioned at the beginning of the Surah—in another form:

"Indeed, these [people] say," (إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ لَيَقُولُونَ‌).

"There is nothing but our first death, and we shall not be resurrected" (إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا مَوْتَتُنَا الْأُولى‌) [1]

[1]: Regarding what the pronoun "it" (هِيَ) refers to, some commentators have traced it back to "death" (موتة), which is understood from the context of the speech; thus, the meaning of the verse is: "Death is nothing but our first death" (Tafsir al-Tibyan, Majma' al-Bayan, and al-Kashshaf). Meanwhile, others consider the referent of the pronoun to be "the end" or "the outcome" (عاقبة و نهاية); thus, the meaning is: "The end of our affair is nothing but the first death" (Ruh al-Ma'ani and al-Mizan). Of course, in terms of the ultimate conclusion, there is not much difference.

And whatever Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله says regarding the Resurrection, life after death, reward and punishment, and Paradise and Hell—none of it is reality; there is no gathering or dispersal at all! Here, a question arises: why do the "polytheists" emphasize only the first death, the implication of which is the non-existence of another death after this one, while their intention is to negate life after death, not to deny a second death? In other words, the Prophets gave news of life after death, not news of a second death.

In response, we say the intention is the non-existence of any other state after death. That is, we die only once and everything ends, and after that, there is neither another life nor another death; whatever exists is just this one death (reflect carefully). [1] In reality, the concept of this verse bears a great resemblance to the concept of verse 29 of Surah Al-An'am, which says: وَ قالُوا إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا حَياتُنَا الدُّنْيا وَ ما نَحْنُ بِمَبْعُوثِينَ‌: "And they say: 'There is nothing but our worldly life, and we shall not be resurrected'!"

Then, it quotes their words where they resorted to a hollow and baseless argument to prove their claim, saying: "Then bring back our forefathers, if you are truthful [that there is a life after death]!" (فَأْتُوا بِآبائِنا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ‌). Some have said the speaker of these words was "Abu Jahl," who turned to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and said: "If you are truthful, revive your ancestor 'Qusayy ibn Kilab,' because he

[1]: Commentators have mentioned other possibilities in the interpretation of this sentence, all of which seem unlikely. Among them is interpreting "the first death" as "the death before the life of this world." Thus, the meaning of the sentence becomes: "The death after which there is life is only the death we had previously when we were all dust, but as for our second death, there is never any life after it."

was a truthful man, so that we may ask him about the events after death." [1] It is obvious that these were all excuses. Although the Divine Sunnah (tradition) is not to revive the dead in this world so they may bring news of that world to this one, even if such a thing had been performed by the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, these excuse-seekers would have struck another tune and called it, for example, another magic—just as they repeatedly asked for miracles and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله showed them, yet they still denied.

Key Point: The Belief of the Polytheists Regarding the Resurrection

The polytheists, especially the Arab polytheists, did not have a uniform approach to their doctrinal issues. While they shared the principle of the doctrine of polytheism (shirk), they differed greatly in the specifics of their beliefs.

One group accepted neither God nor the Resurrection. These are the ones whose words the Quran quotes as such: ما هِيَ إِلَّا حَياتُنَا الدُّنْيا نَمُوتُ وَ نَحْيا وَ ما يُهْلِكُنا إِلَّا الدَّهْرُ: "There is nothing but this worldly life; some die and another generation comes into being, and nothing destroys us but time!" (Al-Jathiya: 24).

Another group accepted God and considered idols as intercessors before Him, but they denied the Resurrection. These were the ones who said: مَنْ يُحْيِ الْعِظامَ وَ هِيَ رَمِيمٌ‌: "Who can give life to bones when they are decayed?" (Ya-Sin: 78). They performed Hajj for the idols, offered sacrifices, and believed in the lawful and the prohibited; the majority of Arab polytheists fell into this category.

[1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 66, and some other commentaries.

However, there are numerous pieces of evidence showing that they believed in some form of the soul's survival, whether in the form of reincarnation and the transfer of souls into new bodies or in another form. [1] Specifically, their belief in a bird called "Hamah" is well-known. It is mentioned in Arabic legends that there were those among the Arabs who believed the human soul is a bird that is spread throughout his body; when the person dies or is killed, it comes out of his body and circumambulates his corpse in a terrifying manner and wails beside his grave. They believed that this bird is small at first, then grows until it becomes the size of an owl, and it lives in constant terror, and its dwelling place is empty houses, ruins, graves, and the places of the slain!

They also believed that if someone had been murdered, the "Hamah" would cry out over his grave: "Isquni fa-inni sadiyah!" (Give me drink, for I am intensely thirsty!). [2] Islam drew a line of cancellation over all these superstitious beliefs. Therefore, it is narrated in a famous Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that he said: "La Hamah" (The Hamah is a lie). 3 In any case, although they did not believe in the Resurrection and the return of man to life after death, it seems they held to a type of reincarnation and the survival of souls.

But in any case, they completely denied the physical Resurrection as the Quran describes—where the dust of man is gathered once more and returns to a new life, and both soul and body share a common resurrection. Not only did they deny it, but they were terrified by it, which the Quran has explained and proven to them through various statements.

[1]: Sharh Nahj al-Balagha by Ibn Abi al-Hadid, Vol. 1, p. 117.

[2]: Bulugh al-Arab, Vol. 2, p. 311.

Ayahs

أَهُمْ خَيْرٌ أَمْ قَوْمُ تُبَّعٍ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ مُجْرِمِينَ (٣٧)
Are they better, or the people of Tubba‘, and those who were before them? We destroyed them; indeed, they were guilty. (37)
وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَـٰعِبِينَ (٣٨)
We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them for play. (38)
مَا خَلَقْنَـٰهُمَآ إِلَّا بِٱلْحَقِّ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٩)
We did not create them except with consummate wisdom; but most of them do not know. (39)

Commentary

Are They Better, or the People of "Tubba'"?!

The land of Yemen, located in the south of the Arabian Peninsula, is among the prosperous and blessed lands that was the cradle of a brilliant civilization in the past. It was ruled by kings known as "Tubba'" (plural: "Tababi'ah"), so named because the people "followed" (taba'iyyat) them, or because they succeeded one another in power.

In any case, the "People of Tubba'" were a population of great power and strength, possessing a vast and expansive government.

In the above verses, following the discussion regarding the polytheists of Makkah and their stubbornness and denial of the Resurrection, Allah threatens them by referring to the story of the "People of Tubba'." He warns them that not only does divine punishment await them in the Hereafter, but in this world, they will also face a fate similar to that of the sinful and disbelieving People of Tubba'.

He says: "Are they better, or the people of Tubba' and those before them? We destroyed them, for they were indeed criminals" (أَ هُمْ خَيْرٌ أَمْ قَوْمُ تُبَّعٍ وَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ أَهْلَكْناهُمْ إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا مُجْرِمِينَ).

It is clear that the people of Hijaz were more or less informed about the history of the "People of Tubba'" who lived in their neighborhood; therefore, the verse does not provide further details about them. It says enough to warn: fear that you might meet a fate like theirs and like other nations that lived around you on your route to Sham (Syria) and in the land of Egypt near you.

Even if you deny the Resurrection, can you deny the punishments that descended upon these criminal and rebellious nations?!

The phrase "those before them" (الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ) refers to nations such as the people of Nuh, 'Ad, and Thamud.

Regarding the People of Tubba', we will discuss them in the "Key Points" section, God willing.

Then, the discourse returns once more to the issue of the Resurrection, proving this reality with a subtle argument, saying: "We did not create the heavens and the earth and all that is between them in play (without purpose)" (وَ ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ) [1].

[1]: "La'ib" (player/in play) is derived from "La'b." According to Raghib, it refers to an action performed without a correct purpose. Additionally, it should be noted that the dual form in "ma baynahuma" (between the two) is because it refers to the categories of heaven and earth.

Indeed, this vast and magnificent creation had a purpose. If, as you claim, death is the end of life, and after a few days of eating, sleeping, lust, and animalistic desires, life concludes and everything ends, then this creation would be mere play, vanity, and futility.

It is unbelievable that the All-Powerful and All-Wise Allah would create this massive system only for these few days of fleeting, aimless life, accompanied by various pains and sufferings. This would never be consistent with the Wisdom of Allah.

Therefore, observing the state of this world shows that it is an entrance and a hallway to a greater and eternal world. Why do you not reflect on this?

The Quran has repeatedly stated this truth in various Surahs. In Surah Al-Anbiya, verse 16, it says regarding this: وَ ما خَلَقْنَا السَّماءَ وَ الْأَرْضَ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما لاعِبِينَ.

And in Surah Al-Waqi'ah, verse 62, it says: وَ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ النَّشْأَةَ الْأُولى‌ فَلَوْ لا تَذَكَّرُونَ (And you have already known the first creation, so why do you not take heed?).

In any case, the creation of this world becomes purposeful only if there is another world following it. For this reason, atheistic schools of thought and deniers of the Resurrection believe in the futility and absurdity of creation.

Then, to emphasize this point, He adds: "We did not create them except in truth" (ما خَلَقْناهُما إِلَّا بِالْحَقِ‌).

The "truthfulness" of this system necessitates that it has a rational goal, which is impossible without the existence of another world. Furthermore, its being "in truth" requires that the righteous and the wicked not be equal. Since we rarely see in this world that each of these two groups receives the appropriate recompense for their deeds, "truth" necessitates that there be an accounting, a book, reward, and punishment in another world, so that everyone receives the appropriate recompense for their actions.

In short, "Truth" (Haqq) in this verse refers to the correct purpose of creation, the testing of humans, the law of perfection, and the implementation of the principles of justice.

"But most of them do not know" (وَ لكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ‌).

This is because they do not employ their intellect and thought; otherwise, the proofs for the Origin (Allah) and the Return (Resurrection) are clear and manifest.

Key Point: Who Were the People of Tubba'?

The word "Tubba'" appears in only two places in the Holy Quran: once in the verses under discussion and the other in Surah Qaf, verse 14, where it says: وَ أَصْحابُ الْأَيْكَةِ وَ قَوْمُ تُبَّعٍ كُلٌّ كَذَّبَ الرُّسُلَ فَحَقَّ وَعِيدِ: "And the companions of the thicket (the people of Shu'ayb) and the people of Tubba'. Each one denied the messengers, so My threat was fulfilled."

As previously mentioned, "Tubba'" was a general title for the kings and monarchs of Yemen, similar to "Khosrow" (Kisra) for the kings of Persia, "Khagan" for the Turkish kings, "Pharaoh" (Fir'awn) for the kings of Egypt, and "Caesar" (Qaysar) for the kings of Rome.

This term (Tubba') was applied to the kings of Yemen because they invited the people to follow them, or because they succeeded one another.

However, it appears that the Quran is speaking about one specific king of Yemen (just as the Pharaoh contemporary with Musa عليه السلام, whom the Quran speaks of, was a specific individual). In some narrations, it is mentioned that his name was "As'ad Abu Karib."

A group of commentators believe that he personally was a seeker of truth and a believer. They take the expression "People of Tubba'" in the two Quranic verses as evidence for this meaning, because in these two verses, he himself is not dispraised; rather, his people are dispraised.

A narration reported from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله also supports this meaning. In this narration, we read that he said:

"Do not insult Tubba', for he had embraced Islam." [1]

In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated:

"Tubba' said to the Aws and Khazraj: 'Stay here until this Prophet emerges. As for me, if I were to reach his time, I would serve him and rise with him!'" [2]

In another narration, it is mentioned that when "Tubba'" came near "Madinah" during one of his world-conquering expeditions, he sent a message to the Jewish scholars residing in that land, saying: "I will destroy this land so that no Jew remains in it, and the religion of the Arabs shall prevail here."

"Shamul" the Jew, who was the most learned of the Jewish scholars there, said: "O King, this is a city that is the place of migration for a Prophet from the progeny of Isma'il who will be born in Makkah."
