He then listed some of the attributes of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. "Tubba'," who apparently had prior knowledge of this, said: "Therefore, I shall not proceed with the destruction of this city." [3]

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 66 (under the verse being discussed). A similar meaning is also narrated in Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur and also in Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 25, p. 116. [2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses being discussed. [3]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 25, p. 118.

It is even mentioned in a narration following this same story that he ordered some of the tribes of Aws and Khazraj who were with him: "Stay in this city, and when the promised Prophet emerges, assist him, and enjoin your children to do the same." He even wrote a letter and entrusted it to them, in which he declared his faith in the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. [1]

The author of A'lam al-Qur'an narrates: "Tubba' was one of the world-conquering kings of Yemen who campaigned as far as India and conquered all the countries of that region. During one of his campaigns, he entered Makkah and intended to destroy the Kaaba. He was struck by a severe illness that the physicians were unable to cure. Among his attendants was a group of scholars, and their leader was a sage named 'Shamul.' He said: 'Your illness is due to your evil intention regarding the House of the Kaaba, and whenever you turn away from this thought and seek forgiveness, you will find healing.' Tubba' turned back from his decision and vowed to honor the House of the Kaaba. When he recovered, he covered the Kaaba with a garment of Yemeni cloth (Burd)."

"In other histories, the story of the covering of the Kaaba has been narrated to the extent of being mutawatir (widely transmitted). This expedition and the matter of covering the Kaaba occurred in the fifth century AD, and currently, in the city of Makkah, there is a place called Dar al-Tababi'ah." [2]

However, in any case, the major part of the history of the Tababi'ah kings of Yemen is not without ambiguity from a historical perspective, as we do not have much information regarding their number or the duration of their rule, and we sometimes encounter contradictory narrations in this regard. What is mostly discussed in Islamic books—including Tafsir, history, and Hadith—revolves around the same sultan to whom the Quran refers in two instances.

[1]: Same source. [2]: A'lam al-Qur'an, pp. 257–259 (summarized).

Ayahs

إِنَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْفَصْلِ مِيقَـٰتُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٤٠)
Indeed the Day of Judgement is the tryst for them all, (40)
يَوْمَ لَا يُغْنِى مَوْلًى عَن مَّوْلًى شَيْـًٔا وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ (٤١)
the day when a friend will not avail a friend in any way, nor will they be helped, (41)
إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٤٢)
except for him on whom Allah has mercy. Indeed He is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (42)

Commentary

The Day of Separations! (Yawm al-Fasl)

The verses under discussion are, in fact, the conclusion of the previous verses regarding the issue of Resurrection, in which the existence of the Day of Judgment was argued through the "wisdom of the creation of this world."

In the first verse, He draws a conclusion from this reasoning as follows: "Indeed, the Day of Separation is the appointed time for them all" (إِنَّ يَوْمَ الْفَصْلِ مِيقاتُهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ).

What a fascinating expression for the Day of Resurrection: "Yawm al-Fasl" (The Day of Separation)—the day when truth is separated from falsehood, the ranks of the righteous and the wicked are distinguished, and man is separated even from his closest friends. Yes, that day is the appointed time for all criminals. [1]

Then, He provides a brief description of this day of separation, saying: "The Day when no friend will avail a friend in the least, nor shall they be helped" (يَوْمَ لا يُغْنِي مَوْلًى عَنْ مَوْلًى شَيْئاً وَ لا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ). Indeed, that day is the day of separation and parting; it is a day when man is separated from everything except his deeds. "Mawla," in whatever sense it may be—friend, guardian, benefactor, relative, neighbor, helper, and the like—has no power to solve the smallest of problems among the difficulties of the Resurrection for anyone.

"Mawla" is derived from the root "Wala’," which originally means the connection of two things with each other such that no stranger stands between them. There are many instances of this mentioned in lexicons as various meanings of this word, all of which share the same root and original meaning. [2]

[1]: Regarding who the pronoun in "miqatuhum" (their appointed time) refers to, commentators have offered various possibilities: some have returned it to all human beings, and some specifically to the nations mentioned in the previous verses, namely the people of Tubba' and the tyrants before them; however, the first meaning seems more correct.

[2]: Many meanings have been mentioned for "Mawla" in linguistics, which some consider to reach twenty-seven meanings: 1- Lord (Rabb) 2- Paternal uncle 3- Cousin 4- Son 5- Nephew (sister's son) 6- Emancipator 7- Freed slave 8- Slave 9- Owner 10- Follower 11- Recipient of a favor 12- Partner 13- Ally 14- Friend 15- Neighbor 16- Guest 17- Son-in-law 18- Relatives 19- Bestower of blessings 20- One who is lost 21- Guardian 22- One who is more worthy 23- Master (Agha) 24- Lover 25- Helper 26- One with authority of disposal 27- Trustee (Al-Ghadir, Vol. 1, p. 362).

Not only do friends not come to each other's aid, and relatives do not untie each other's knots, but also plans are frustrated, remedies reach a dead end, and arrows miss their mark, as we read in verse 46 of Surah At-Tur: يَوْمَ لا يُغْنِي عَنْهُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْئاً وَ لا هُمْ يُنْصَرُونَ: "The Day when their plotting will not avail them at all, nor will they be helped."

Regarding the difference between "la yughni" (will not avail) and "la hum yunsarun" (nor shall they be helped), the best explanation is to say: the first refers to the fact that no one can solve another's problem alone and independently on that day, and the second refers to the fact that they cannot even solve problems through mutual cooperation, because "Nusrat" (help/victory) is used when a person rushes to the aid of another to overcome problems through cooperation.

Only one group is excluded, as the next verse says: "Except those on whom Allah has mercy, for He is the Almighty, the Merciful" (إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ اللَّهُ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ). Undoubtedly, this Divine mercy is not arbitrary; it only includes the believers who possess righteous deeds, and if a slip has occurred from them, it is not to the extent that it has severed their bonds with Allah. These are the ones who cling to the mantle of Divine grace, benefit from the sea of His generosity, are quenched by the spring of His mercy, and enjoy the intercession of His Awliya (saints).

From this, it becomes clear that the negation of any friend, guardian, or helper on that day does not contradict the issue of intercession (Shafa'at), because intercession also does not occur except by the permission of the Lord and His command.

It is interesting that the descriptions of being "Almighty" (Aziz) and "Merciful" (Rahim) are placed together; the first refers to the ultimate power and His invincibility, and the second refers to His infinite mercy. The important point is that mercy exists alongside power.

In some narrations from the Ahl al-Bayt عليه السلام, it is narrated that the meaning of the phrase "إِلَّا مَنْ رَحِمَ اللَّهُ" (Except those on whom Allah has mercy) refers to the successor of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the Commander of the Faithful عليه السلام, and the followers of his school. [1]

[1]: "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 4, p. 629. It goes without saying that the goal is to state a clear manifestation (misdaq).

Ayahs

إِنَّ شَجَرَتَ ٱلزَّقُّومِ (٤٣)
Indeed the tree of Zaqqum (43)
طَعَامُ ٱلْأَثِيمِ (٤٤)
will be the food of the sinful. (44)
كَٱلْمُهْلِ يَغْلِى فِى ٱلْبُطُونِ (٤٥)
Like molten copper it will boil in their bellies, (45)
كَغَلْىِ ٱلْحَمِيمِ (٤٦)
seething like boiling water. (46)
خُذُوهُ فَٱعْتِلُوهُ إِلَىٰ سَوَآءِ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٤٧)
[The keepers of hell will be told,] ‘Seize him and drag him to the middle of hell, (47)
ثُمَّ صُبُّوا۟ فَوْقَ رَأْسِهِۦ مِنْ عَذَابِ ٱلْحَمِيمِ (٤٨)
then pour over his head boiling water as punishment, (48)
ذُقْ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْكَرِيمُ (٤٩)
[and tell them,] “Taste this! Indeed you are the [self-styled] mighty and noble! (49)
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا مَا كُنتُم بِهِۦ تَمْتَرُونَ (٥٠)
This is what you used to doubt!’ (50)

Commentary

The Tree of Zaqqum!

In these verses, a terrifying and shaking description of the punishments of Hell is reflected, which completes the discussion of the previous verses regarding the "Day of Sorting" and the Day of Resurrection.

He says: "The tree of Zaqqum..." (إِنَّ شَجَرَةَ الزَّقُّومِ).

"...is the food of the sinful!" (طَعامُ الْأَثِيمِ). It is these people who eat from this bitter, foul-smelling, foul-tasting, and lethal plant.

"Zaqqum," as we mentioned in the commentary of verse 62 of Surah As-Saffat, is—according to commentators and lexicographers—the name of a bitter, foul-smelling, and foul-tasting plant with small leaves that grows in the land of "Tihamah" in the Arabian Peninsula, and the polytheists were familiar with it. It is a plant with a bitter sap that causes swelling when it touches the body. [^1] Some believe it originally means "to swallow," [^2] and others say it means "any kind of loathsome food for the people of Hell." [^3] In a hadith, it is mentioned that when this word was revealed in the Quran, the pagans of Quraysh said, "Such a plant does not exist in our land; who among you knows the meaning of 'Zaqqum'?" A man from Africa was there and said: "Zaqqum in our language means..."

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan, Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani. [^2]: Lisan al-Arab, root "z-q-m". [^3]: Mufradat al-Raghib, root "z-q-m".

"...butter and dates" (perhaps his intention was also mockery). When Abu Jahl heard this, he called out mockingly, "O slave-girl! Bring some butter and dates so we may 'Zaqqum' it!" They ate and mocked, saying, "Muhammad frightens us with these." [^1] Additionally, it should be noted that "shajarah" in the Arabic language and Quranic usage sometimes means "tree" and sometimes refers to "plants" in general.

"Athim" is derived from "ithm," meaning one who persists in sin, and here it refers to the stubborn, transgressive, and sinful disbelievers.

Then He adds: "Like molten metal, it boils in the bellies of the sinners!" (كَالْمُهْلِ يَغْلِي فِي الْبُطُونِ).

"A boiling like that of scalding water" (كَغَلْيِ الْحَمِيمِ). "Muhl," according to many commentators and lexicographers, is molten and glowing metal, and according to others like Raghib in Al-Mufradat, it means "the dregs and sediment of oil," which is something very undesirable; however, the first meaning seems more appropriate.

"Hamim" means hot and boiling water, and sometimes it is applied to intimate and "warm" friends, but here the first meaning is intended.

In any case, when the Zaqqum plant enters their bellies, it creates a state of extraordinary heat and boils like boiling water. Instead of this food being a source of strength and power, it creates misery, punishment, pain, and suffering.

Then He says: The guards of Hell are addressed: "Seize this sinful disbeliever..."

[^1]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, Vol. 8, p. 5529 (under verse 62 of Surah As-Saffat).

"...and drag him into the midst of Hell!" (خُذُوهُ فَاعْتِلُوهُ إِلى‌ سَواءِ الْجَحِيمِ).

"Fa'tiluhu" is from the root "atl" (on the weight of qatl), meaning to seize, drag, and throw—an action that guards perform with rebellious criminals who do not submit to any law.

"Sawa'" means the middle, because its distance to the sides is equal. Taking such individuals to the middle of Hell is because the heat is naturally more intense there, and the flames of fire surround them from every side.

Again, He points to another of their painful punishments, saying: "Then pour over his head the torment of scalding water" (ثُمَّ صُبُّوا فَوْقَ رَأْسِهِ مِنْ عَذابِ الْحَمِيمِ). [^1] In this way, they burn from within, and from without, fire encompasses their entire being, and in the midst of the fire, scalding water is poured upon them.

A similar meaning appears in verse 19 of Surah Al-Hajj: "Scalding water will be poured over their heads" (يُصَبُّ مِنْ فَوْقِ رُؤُسِهِمُ الْحَمِيمُ).

And after all these painful physical punishments, their agonizing psychological punishment begins. It is said to this rebellious, sinful, and faithless criminal: "Taste! You are the one who, in your own imagination, was the most powerful and honorable" (ذُقْ إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْكريمُ). It was you who held the poor in chains and bonds, and practiced oppression and tyranny...

[^1]: "Adhab al-Hamim" is an "explanatory genitive" (idafah bayaniyyah), meaning this scalding water is a punishment that is poured upon them.

...against them; you claimed for yourself invincible power and extraordinary respect.

Yes, it was you who committed every crime with all that pride. Now taste the result of your deeds which have been manifested before your eyes. Just as you burned the bodies and souls of people, now your inside and outside burn in the fire of Divine wrath and with scalding water.

In a hadith, it is mentioned that the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله one day took the hand of Abu Jahl and said: "Woe to you, and then woe!" (أولى لك فأولى). Abu Jahl became upset, pulled his hand away, and said: "With what do you threaten me? Neither you nor your Companion (God) are able to do anything to me! In all this land of Mecca, I am the most powerful and honorable!" The above verse refers to this very meaning, saying: when he is cast into the fire of Hell, he is told, "Taste! O powerful and honorable man of the land of Mecca!" [^1]

And in the final verse under discussion, He adds: They are addressed: "This is indeed what you used to doubt" (إِنَّ هذا ما كُنْتُمْ بِهِ تَمْتَرُونَ). How many times in various verses of the Quran, with different types of evidence, were the reality and truth of this day pointed out to you? Did We not tell you: Look at the resurrection in the world of plants—"Thus is the coming forth" (كَذلِكَ الْخُرُوجُ)—your resurrection is just like this?! (Qaf: 11)

[^1]: Tafsir al-Maraghi, Vol. 25, p. 135 (under the verses discussed), Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, and Tafsir al-Kabir by Fakhr Razi.

Did We not say: Just as rain gives life to dead lands, your gathering and resurrection is just as simple?! "Thus is the resurrection" (كَذلِكَ النُّشُورُ) (Fatir: 9).

Did We not say: The matter of reviving the dead is very easy for Allah?! "And that is easy for Allah" (وَ ذلِكَ عَلَى اللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ) (Taghabun: 7).

Did We not say: Was the first creation difficult for Us that you doubt the resurrection? "Were We then fatigued by the first creation?" (أَ فَعَيِينا بِالْخَلْقِ الْأَوَّلِ) (Surah Qaf, verse 15).

In short, the truth was told to you through various ways; alas, you did not have a listening ear!

Key Point: Physical and Spiritual Punishments

We know that according to the explicit text of the Holy Quran, the Hereafter has both physical and spiritual aspects. Naturally, the punishments and rewards must also possess both aspects. Therefore, in the verses of the Quran and Islamic narrations, both parts are mentioned. However, since the attention of the masses is more toward physical aspects, more description and explanation are seen regarding physical punishments and rewards, but the references to spiritual reward and punishment are also not few.

We saw an example of this in the above verses where, along with listing several parts of painful physical punishments, a short and meaningful reference was also made to the spiritual punishment of the arrogant rebels.

In other verses of the Quran, references to spiritual rewards are also seen. In one place He says: "And the pleasure of Allah is greater" (وَ رِضْوانٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ أَكْبَرُ)—the pleasure and satisfaction of Allah is superior to all these rewards (Tawbah: 72).

In another place He says: "Peace! A word from a Merciful Lord" (سَلامٌ قَوْلًا مِنْ رَبٍّ رَحِيمٍ)—for them is peace and greeting from the Merciful and Compassionate Lord (Ya-Sin: 58).

And finally, in another place He says: "And We shall remove whatever rancor is in their breasts; as brothers, [they shall be] on couches, facing each other" (وَ نَزَعْنا ما فِي صُدُورِهِمْ مِنْ غِلٍّ إِخْواناً عَلى‌ سُرُرٍ مُتَقابِلِينَ)—We remove any kind of envy, malice, and enmity from their hearts; all are brothers and are placed on thrones facing one another (Al-Hijr: 47).

It goes without saying that spiritual pleasures, especially in that vast and expansive world, are often indescribable; hence, in the verses of the Quran, they are usually referred to in a general manner. As for spiritual punishments, they are reflected in the form of humiliations, reproaches, regrets, and sorrows, an example of which we read in the above verses.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ فِى مَقَامٍ أَمِينٍ (٥١)
Indeed the Godwary will be in a safe place, (51)
فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (٥٢)
amid gardens and springs, (52)
يَلْبَسُونَ مِن سُندُسٍ وَإِسْتَبْرَقٍ مُّتَقَـٰبِلِينَ (٥٣)
dressed in fine silk and brocade, sitting face to face. (53)
كَذَٰلِكَ وَزَوَّجْنَـٰهُم بِحُورٍ عِينٍ (٥٤)
So shall it be, and We shall wed them to black-eyed houris. (54)
يَدْعُونَ فِيهَا بِكُلِّ فَـٰكِهَةٍ ءَامِنِينَ (٥٥)
Secure [from any kind of harm,] there they will call for every kind of fruit [they wish]. (55)
لَا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا ٱلْمَوْتَ إِلَّا ٱلْمَوْتَةَ ٱلْأُولَىٰ ۖ وَوَقَىٰهُمْ عَذَابَ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٥٦)
Other than the first death, they will not taste death therein, and He will save them from the punishment of hell (56)
فَضْلًا مِّن رَّبِّكَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٥٧)
—a grace from your Lord. That is the great success. (57)

Commentary

The Pious and the Varieties of Heavenly Bounties

Since the preceding verses discussed the painful punishments of the people of Hell, these verses enumerate the favors and rewards of the people of Paradise, so that through the principle of contrast, the significance of each may become more apparent.

These rewards are summarized in seven parts:

First: "The pious are in a position of security" (إِنَّ الْمُتَّقِينَ فِي مَقامٍ أَمِينٍ) [^1]. Therefore, no distress or insecurity reaches them, and they live in complete safety from afflictions and calamities, from grief and sorrow, and from devils and tyrants.

Then, moving to the second bounty, He says: "They are amid gardens and springs, and their place is surrounded on all sides by trees and fountains" (فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَ عُيُونٍ). The use of the plural "Jannat" (gardens full of trees) may refer to the multiplicity of gardens that each inhabitant of Paradise possesses, or it may refer to their varying ranks, as the gardens of Paradise are not all identical and differ according to the degrees of the inhabitants.

[^1]: It is noteworthy that "Amin" (secure/trustworthy) is mentioned as an attribute for the "Maqam" (position/place), as if the place of the people of Paradise is itself trustworthy and will commit no betrayal against them; such expressions are usually used for emphasis and hyperbole.

In the third stage, He refers to their beautiful garments, adding: "They wear garments of fine silk and heavy brocade, facing one another" (يَلْبَسُونَ مِنْ سُندُسٍ وَ إِسْتَبْرَقٍ مُتَقابِلِينَ).

"Sundus" refers to thin and delicate silk fabrics, and some have added the condition of being woven with gold.

"Istabraq" means thick silk fabrics, and a group of linguists and commentators consider it the Arabized form of the Persian word "Istabr" or "Stabr" (meaning thick). There is also the possibility that its root is Arabic, derived from "Barq" (meaning luster/brilliance), because of the special radiance these types of fabrics possess.

Of course, in Paradise, there is no extreme heat or cold to be repelled by wearing clothes; rather, these refer to the diverse and varied heavenly garments. As we have said before, our words and terms, which were established to meet needs in the daily life of this world, are incapable of describing the matters of that great and perfect world; they can only serve as indications toward it.

Some have also considered the difference in these garments as an indication of the difference in the rank of proximity of the inhabitants of Paradise.

Furthermore, the fact that the inhabitants of Paradise face one another and the removal of any difference or seeking of superiority among them is an indication of the spirit of intimacy and brotherhood that prevails in their gatherings—gatherings where nothing exists in the atmosphere except purity, spirituality, and transcendence.

In the fourth stage, it is the turn of their spouses; He says: "Thus it is, and We shall wed them to Hur al-’In" (كَذلِكَ وَ زَوَّجْناهُمْ بِحُورٍ عِينٍ).

"Hur" is the plural of "Hawra" and "Ahwar," referring to someone whose pupils are intensely black and whose whites are perfectly transparent.

"‘In" (rhyming with "Chin") is the plural of "A’yan" and "‘Ayna," meaning large-eyed.

Since human beauty is most evident in the eyes, He describes the beautiful eyes of the Hur al-’In here; of course, in other verses of the Quran, their other beauties are also presented in a "beautiful manner."

Then, mentioning the fifth bounty of the people of Paradise, He adds: "They will call therein for every kind of fruit, in peace and security" (يَدْعُونَ فِيها بِكُلِّ فاكِهَةٍ آمِنِينَ). Even the difficulties that exist in benefiting from the fruits of this world will not exist for them. The fruits are all near and accessible; thus, the toil and pain of picking fruit from tall trees is not there: "Its clusters of fruit hanging low" (Al-Haqqah: 23).

The choice of whatever fruit they desire is in their hands: "And fruit of what they select" (Al-Waqi'ah: 20).

The illnesses and discomforts that sometimes arise from eating fruits in this world do not exist there, nor is there any fear of their decay, shortage, or exhaustion. In every respect, their minds are at ease and in security.

In any case, if the food of the people of Hell is "Zaqqum" and boils within them like scalding water, the food of the people of Paradise is delightful fruits free from any discomfort.

The eternity of Paradise and heavenly bounties is the sixth divine favor upon the pious, because what troubles the human mind at the time of union is the fear of separation. Therefore, He says: "They shall not taste death therein except the first death [which they tasted in the world]" (لا يَذُوقُونَ فِيهَا الْمَوْتَ إِلَّا الْمَوْتَةَ الْأُولى).

Interestingly, the Quran expresses the issue of the eternity of heavenly bounties with various terms; sometimes it says: "Abiding therein forever" [^1] and sometimes it says: "A gift uninterrupted" (Hud: 108).

Regarding why it is expressed as "the first death" (al-mawtat al-ula), there are points that will be discussed in the "Key Points" section.

Finally, He describes the seventh and last bounty in this series as follows:

"And He has protected them from the punishment of the Blazing Fire" (وَ وَقاهُمْ عَذابَ الْجَحِيمِ).

The perfection of these bounties lies in the fact that the possibility of punishment and the thought of penalty does not occupy or worry the minds of the inhabitants of Paradise.

This indicates that if the pious had any slips, Allah, by His grace and generosity, has forgiven them and assured them not to let any worry enter their hearts in this regard. In other words, other than the Infallibles (Ma'sumin), everyone inevitably has slips and remains fearful of them until they are covered by divine forgiveness; this verse grants them complete security in this regard.

A question arises here: some believers remain in Hell for a period of time because of their sins until they are purified and then come to Paradise; does the above verse not apply to them?

In response, it can be said: the verse speaks of high-ranking pious individuals who enter Paradise from the very beginning, and it is silent regarding the other group.

There is also the possibility that this group, after entering Paradise, no longer fears returning to Hell and is in peace and security; that is, the above verse depicts their state after entering Paradise.

In the last verse under discussion, referring to all these seven bounties, He says as a summary: "All this is as a grace from your Lord; that is the supreme success and the great victory that encompasses the pious" (فَضْلًا مِنْ رَبِّكَ ذلِكَ هُوَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ) [^1]. It is true that the pious performed many good deeds in the world, but certainly, the just reward for those meager actions is not all these endless and eternal bounties; it is the grace of Allah that has placed all these rewards at their disposal. Furthermore, if divine grace had not encompassed them in the world, they could never have performed those good deeds; He gave them intellect and knowledge, He sent the divine Prophets and heavenly Books, and He granted them the success (tawfiq) of guidance and action.

Yes, benefiting from these great successes and reaching all those rewards in the light of these successes is a "supreme success" and a great victory that is not achieved except under the shadow of His grace.

[^1]: Regarding the grammatical position (i'rab) of "Fadlan," several possibilities have been given: first, that it is a "cognate accusative" (maf'ul mutlaq) for a hidden verb; second, that it is a "circumstantial accusative" (hal) or "accusative of purpose" (maf'ul lahu).

Key Point: What is the First Death?

In the above verses, we read that the inhabitants of Paradise taste no death except the first death. Here, three questions arise.

First: What is meant by the first death? If it is the death that constitutes the end of worldly life, why does it say: the inhabitants of Paradise "shall not taste" (future/present tense) except the first death, while they have already tasted it (and it should be said in the past tense)?

In response to this question, some have taken "illa" (except) in (إِلَّا الْمَوْتَةَ الْأُولى) to mean "after" and said the meaning of the verse is that after the first death, they will taste no other death.

Some have assumed an ellipsis here and said the structure is: "Except the first death which they have already tasted" [^1].

Another question: Why is only the first death mentioned when we know that man tastes two deaths: a death at the end of worldly life, and a death after the life of Barzakh (the intermediate realm)?

In response to this question, answers have been given, none of which are acceptable, and we see no need to mention them. It is better to say that the life and death of Barzakh bear no resemblance to ordinary life and death; rather, according to the requirements of physical resurrection, the life of the Resurrection is similar in some respects to the life of this world, but at a much higher and loftier level. Therefore, the inhabitants of Paradise are told that except for the first death you had in the world, there will be no other death. And because the life and death of Barzakh absolutely do not resemble it, no mention is made of it [^1].

The third question: The absence of death in the Resurrection is not exclusive to the inhabitants of Paradise; the people of Hell also do not die. So why is the emphasis placed on the inhabitants of Paradise in the verse?

The late Tabarsi in "Majma' al-Bayan" has an interesting answer; he says: This is so it may be a glad tiding for the inhabitants of Paradise that they have a pleasant eternal life. But for the people of Hell, for whom every moment of their life is a death and who are as if they are constantly dying and being revived, this statement has no meaning.

In any case, the expression "la yadhuqun" (they shall not taste) here indicates that even the slightest traces of death will not appear for the inhabitants of Paradise.

Interestingly, in a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "On the Day of Resurrection, Allah says to some of the inhabitants of Paradise: 'By My Might and My Majesty, and My Sublimity and the loftiness of My station, I shall surely grant him five things today... they are youths who do not grow old, healthy ones who do not fall ill, wealthy ones who will not become poor, happy ones who will not grieve, and living ones who do not die.' Then he recited this verse: 'They shall not taste death therein except the first death.'" [^2]

[^1]: Therefore, the above exception is also a "disconnected exception" (istithna' munqati'), because the inhabitants of Paradise do not taste such a death [in Paradise], but rather have tasted it previously (take note).

[^1]: We have discussed the life and death of Barzakh in detail in Volume 20 under verse 11 of Surah al-Mu'min.

[^2]: "Usul al-Kafi" as quoted in "Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 4, p. 634.

Ayahs

فَإِنَّمَا يَسَّرْنَـٰهُ بِلِسَانِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (٥٨)
Indeed We have made it simple in your language, so that they may take admonition. (58)
فَٱرْتَقِبْ إِنَّهُم مُّرْتَقِبُونَ (٥٩)
So wait! They [too] are waiting. (59)

Commentary

Wait for it, for they too are waiting!

We have stated that "Surah ad-Dukhan" began by describing the greatness and depth of the Quranic verses, and it concludes with the above verses which also reflect the profound impact of the Quranic verses, so that the beginning of the Surah harmonizes with its end. Whatever has been stated between this beginning and end is also an emphasis on the exhortations and admonitions of the Quran.

He says: "Indeed, We have made this Quran easy in your tongue so that they may take heed" (فَإِنَّما يَسَّرْناهُ بِلِسانِكَ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَذَكَّرُونَ‌).

Despite its content being exceptionally deep and its dimensions being very vast, it is simple, fluent, understandable for everyone, and usable for all strata of society. Its parables are beautiful, its metaphors are natural and eloquent, its stories are true and instructive, its proofs are clear and firm, its expression is simple, concise, and rich in content, and at the same time sweet and attractive, so that it may penetrate the depths of human hearts, awaken the unaware, and remind the ready hearts.

Some commentators have mentioned another interpretation for this verse, according to which the meaning is: despite the fact that you are unschooled, you can easily and comfortably recite these rich verses which represent divine revelation and miracles.

However, the first interpretation seems more appropriate, and in fact, this verse is similar to the verse that has been repeated several times in Surah al-Qamar: وَ لَقَدْ يَسَّرْنَا الْقُرْآنَ لِلذِّكْرِ فَهَلْ مِنْ مُدَّكِرٍ: "And We have certainly made the Quran easy for remembrance, so is there any who will remember?"

But since, despite all these qualities, a group still does not submit to the word of Truth, He threatens them in the final verse, saying: "If, despite all this, they do not accept, then wait; indeed, they too are waiting!" (فارْتَقِبْ إِنَّهُمْ مُرْتَقِبُونَ‌).

You wait for the divine promises regarding victory over the disbelievers, and let them wait for defeat. You wait for the painful divine punishment for this stubborn and oppressive people, while they, in their own imagination, wait for your defeat and failure, until it becomes clear which of these two expectations is correct.

Therefore, one should never conclude from this verse that Allah is commanding His Prophet to completely stop preaching to them, to cease his efforts and endeavors, and to be content only with waiting; rather, this is a type of threat used to awaken stubborn individuals.

Key Points

1- "Artaqib" (ارتقب) is originally derived from "raqabah" (on the pattern of 'talabah') meaning "neck." Since individuals who are waiting for something constantly stretch their necks, it has come to mean waiting and watching over something.

2- The above verses clearly show that the Holy Quran does not belong to a specific stratum or group; rather, it is for the understanding, remembrance, and admonition of the general public. Therefore, those who place the Quran within the twists and turns of ambiguous concepts and incomprehensible issues—claiming its understanding belongs only to a specific class, while even that class understands nothing of it—are in reality heedless of the spirit of the Quran.

The Quran must be present in the lives of all people: in the city and the village, in solitude and in gatherings, in primary schools and universities, in the mosque and on the battlefield, and everywhere, because "Allah has made it easy, simple, and fluent so that everyone may take heed."

Furthermore, this verse strikes a blow to the ideas of those who reduce the Quran to the style of recitation and the complexities of the rules of Tajwid, and whose only concern is the articulation of its words from their phonetic outlets and observing the etiquette of pausing and joining. It says all of this is for "remembrance"—a remembrance that becomes a catalyst for movement and constructiveness in action. Observing the outward forms of the words is correct in its place, but the ultimate goal is the meanings, not the words.

3- In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is narrated:

لو لا تيسيره لما قدر احد من خلقه ان يتلفظ بحرف من القرآن، و انى لهم ذلك و هو كلام من لم يزل و لا يزال!:

"Had Allah not made the Quran easy upon the tongues, none of His creation would have been able to utter a single letter of the Quran; and how could they, when it is the speech of Him who has always been and will always be?" (And such speech has such awe and greatness that uttering it is impossible for servants without divine grace). [1]

O Lord! Place us among those who take heed from the Quran—this great and peerless word of Yours—and who harmonize their lives in all dimensions with it. O Allah! Grant us that security which You bestow upon the God-fearing, and which brings tranquility to their hearts and souls in the face of the storm of events.

O God! Your blessings are innumerable, Your mercy is beyond count, and Your punishment is painful. Our deeds are not such that they would make us worthy of Your grace or keep us away from Your punishment. Treat us with the same grace You promised to the righteous; otherwise, we shall never find our way into the embrace of Your eternal Paradise.

End of Surah ad-Dukhan Tuesday, 25 Rajab al-Murajjab 1405 (April 16, 1985)

[1]: Tafsir "Ruh al-Bayan," Vol. 8, p. 433.
